coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots from carbonized corncobs by electrochemical oxidation and their application in Na-batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2023 Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Rui-lin
Zhao, Zong-bin
Leng, Chang-yu
Li, Yong
Ai, Li-shen
Sun, Yang
Wang, Xu-zhen
Qiu, Jie-shan
coredata.dc:description: Abstract Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention due to their high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups, low biotoxicity and photoluminescence. However, their preparation on a large-scale is still a great challenge because of the high cost and environmental problems, and this seriously limits their practical applications. Carbonized corncobs were used as the starting material for the preparation of the CDs by electrochemical oxidation. Their natural porous structures with well-developed channels allow the electrode to be filled with electrolyte, and the electrochemical oxidation takes place both on the inside and outside surfaces of the corncob, achieving a CD output of 79.8 mg h−1 per gram of electrode material at 1 A. The CDs were combined with graphene oxide (GO) to produce CD/rGO composite aerogels by a hydrothermal method. After heat treatment at 600 °C, the materials obtained were used as the anode in a sodium ion battery, which had a capacity of 263.3 mAh g−1 after 1 000 cycles at 1 A g−1. This work suggests a new way to prepare CDs and possibly expand their range of application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corncob
Carbon dots
Electrochemical synthesis
Sodium ion batteries

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: rawtext articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst pubtype ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172038, 22179017, U1610105).

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: NEW CARBON MATERIALS Volume 38, Issue 2, Apr. 2023 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 Received date: 23 Nov. 2022; Revised date: 03 Mar. 2023 *Corresponding author. E-mail: zbzhao@dlut.edu.cn; jqiu@dlut. edu. cn CopyrightÂ©2023, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-5805(22)60644-9 RESEARCH PAPER Preparation of carbon dots from carbonized corncobs by electrochemical oxidation and their application in Na- batteries Rui-lin Li1, Zong-bin Zhao1,*, Chang-yu Leng1, Yong Li1, Li-shen Ai1, Yang Sun1, Xu-zhen Wang1, Jie- shan Qiu1,2,* 1State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Liaoning Key Laboratory for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China 2College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China Abstract: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention due to their high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups, low biotoxicity and photoluminescence. However, their preparation on a large-scale is still a great challenge because of the high cost and environmental problems, and this seriously limits their practical applications. Carbonized corncobs were used as the starting material for the preparation of the CDs by electrochemical oxidation. Their natural porous structures with well-developed channels allow the electrode to be filled with electrolyte, and the electrochemical oxidation takes place both on the inside and outside surfaces of the corncob, achieving a CD output of 79.8 mg hâˆ’1 per gram of electrode material at 1 A. The CDs were combined with graphene oxide (GO) to produce CD/rGO composite aerogels by a hydrothermal method. After heat treatment at 600 Â°C, the materials obtained were used as the anode in a sodium ion battery, which had a capacity of 263.3 mAh gâˆ’1 after 1 000 cycles at 1 A gâˆ’1. This work suggests a new way to prepare CDs and possibly expand their range of application. Key Words: Corncob; Carbon dots; Electrochemical synthesis; Sodium ion batteries 1 Introduction Generally, carbon dots (CDs) refer to carbon nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm in all three dimensions, composed of carbon cores and surface passive layers with rich functional groups. CDs can be further divided into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and polymer dots (PDs), etc.[1]. Based on their characteristics of high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups and defects, low biotoxicity and unique photoluminescence[1-3], CDs have promising applications in biological imaging[4-5], drug transport[6], electroluminescence devices[7-8], ion detection[9], catalysis[10-11] and other fields. In recent years, CDs have also attracted attention in the construction of high-performance electrode materials for sodium ion batteries[2,12-14]. However, unfortunately, most current methods for preparing CDs are restricted by cost, safety/environmental concerns and other issues, especially pertaining to their large-scale production, greatly hindering the progress towards their practical application. Electrochemical oxidation is a very efficient method for preparing CDs, by which the C-C bond inside the carbon-based material is broken to transform the large carbon precursor into small CDs with the help of hydroxyl radical and oxygen radical generated during this process[15]. The as-prepared CDs are usually endowed with rich oxygen-containing functional groups and excellent dispersion in water. Compared with the traditional chemical liquid phase oxidation method, the use of strong acids and oxidants can be significantly reduced or even eliminated. The electrochemical oxidation is advantageous considering its high safety and controllability, low environmental pollution, fast reaction speed, etc.[16-18]. In 2007, Zhou et al. firstly reported the electrochemical preparation of CDs with multi-walled carbon nanotubes as precursor and acetonitrile solution of [CH3(CH2)3]4NClO4 as electrolyte, CDs with blue fluorescence was obtained under multiple charge- discharge cycles[16]. After that, Li et al. prepared fluorescent color-size-dependent graphene quantum dots by using graphite rod as anode and ethanol/water solution of NaOH as electrolyte for the electrochemical oxidation, and the production rate of CDs reached as high as 10 mg hâ€“1[17]. In 2018, He et al. used coke as raw material and methanol/water solution of (NH4)2S2O8 as electrolyte to produce graphene quantum dots with fluorescence color and size dependent characteristics[18], in which dark yellow dispersion solution can be obtained for 1 h. However, the carbon precursors used in these above methods are synthetic or non-renewable substances, and the electrode requires additional processing and molding, which increases Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 the cost of production. Corn is a widely planted food crop with high-yield. In 2021, China's corn plantation area reached 43.32 million hectares, with an output of 272.55 million tons[19], which not only provided large amount of food and feed for people and livestock but also gave corncobs as byproduct. The main components of corncob are lignin, cellulose and hemicellulose[20,21], therefore, conductive electrode materials can be obtained by direct carbonization. Since corncobs are responsible for the transport of water and nutrients to the grains during the corn growth process, the corncobs have a naturally- developed pore structure, consisting of directional three- dimensional micro and nano pore networks[22]. Thanks to this special structure, carbonized corncob is a suitable electrode material. Under the action of capillary effect, the whole body can be filled with electrolyte naturally. This allows the oxidation process to occur on both the inner and outer surfaces of the carbonized corncob, due to which, both the electrochemical oxidation reaction and CDs preparation rate are significantly improved. In the present work, carbonized corncob with three- dimensional porous structure was obtained by mild pyrolysis using corncob as the raw material. CDs were prepared by electrochemical oxidation method with carbonized corncob as electrode and KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte. The as- obtained CDs possess good water dispersion and show yellowish-green fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. It is revealed that there is a correlation between the size of CDs and the carbonization temperature of electrode precursor employed. The prepared CDs were combined with reduced graphene oxide (rGO), which not only improved the poor conductivity of CDs, but also effectively inhibited the self-stacking of the reduced GO. As an electrode in sodium ion battery it delivered a capacity of 263.3 mAh gâ€“1 after 1 000 cycles at 1 A gâ€“1. This work used carbonized corncob as electrode without any electrode molding process, and the electrode material has a unique three-dimensional porous structure and high efficiency for the preparation of CDs, suggesting new potential pathway for large-scale production. 2 Experimental Section 2.1 Reagents, materials and equipment Analytical grade reagents, such as KOH, concentrated H2SO4, K2SO4, were purchased from Tianjin Kemi Ou Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Platinum wire, diameter about 0.5mm, was purchased from Shanghai Yuehe Electronic Technology Co., LTD. Corncobs were purchased at a market in Dalian, Liaoning Province, China. Analytical grade polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP), and battery grade conductive carbon black, copper foil, were purchased from Shenzhen Kunpeng Electromechanical Co., LTD. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) hydrophilic filter membrane, aperture 0.22 Î¼m, from Haining Yibo Filter Equipment Factory. Dialysis Bag, 200 Da, from Sinopath Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Dc power supply, IT6700, from Idex Electronics LTD. 2.2 Material preparation and device assembly 2.2.1 CDs prepared by electrochemical method The corncob was dried and carbonized for 2 h in a tube furnace at 700-1 000 Â°C, in N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 Â°C minâ€“1. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the samples of carbonized corncob (hereafter denoted as CC-T, where T is the carbonization temperature) was extracted from the furnace for further use. With carbonized corncob as anode, Pt wire as counter electrode and 1 mol Lâ€“1 aqueous KOH solution as electrolyte, a constant current of 1 A was applied at both ends of the electrode, and the dispersion liquid of CDs in the electrolyte was obtained by electrochemical oxidation for 1 h. The electrolyte containing CDs was subjected to vacuum filtration with a 0.22 Î¼m polytetrafluoron hydrophilic filter membrane, and then the filtrate was dialyzed through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 200, until the pH of dialysis precipitation became neutral. The dialysis time was about two days, and the CDs aqueous dispersion was obtained. The CDs aqueous dispersion was freeze dried and subsequently CD powder was obtained. 2.2.2 Preparation of CDs/rGO aerogel composite Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared using a modified Hummer process[23]. 0, 15, 30, 60 mg CDs powder was added into 30 mL GO water dispersion with a concentration of 4 mg mLâ€“1, ultrasonicated for 30 min to make the mixture uniform, and then transferred to 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with PTFE, and kept at 180 Â°C for 24 h. Then the autoclave was allowed to cooled down to room temperature naturally. CDs/rGO composite hydrogel (CDs/ RGO-N, n is the mass ratio of GO to CDs) was taken out, rinsed repeatedly with deionized water for several times, and vacuum dried to obtain CDs/RGO-N aerogel. Then, CDs/RGO-N was heated at 5 Â°C minâ€“1 to 600 Â°C and maintained for 1 h in tubular furnace. After natural cooling, CDs/rGO-n-H aerogel was taken out for use. As for the contrast sample, pure CDs was kept at 600 Â°C for 1 h, named as CDS-H. 2.2.3 Sodium ion button half battery assembly The prepared CDs/rGO-n-H, conductive carbon black and binder PVDF were mixed in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 to prepare a slurry. The slurry was evenly coated on copper foil, and then dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 Â°C for 12 h. The dried electrodes were cut into 14 mm round slices with a button cell manual microtome. The button cell was assembled in a glove box filled with argon (water oxygen content < 0.1Ã—10-6). The prepared electrode was put into CR2016 shell, with sodium metal foil as the electrode and Whatman GF/D wafer with a diameter of 19 mm as the diaphragm. Using 1 mol Lâ€“1 NaPF6 glycol dimethyl ether (DME) solution as electrolyte, the sealed button cell was obtained by pressing in the sealing machine. 2.3 Material characterization The morphology of the materials was characterized by Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 NOVA nano SEM450-type field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and JEM F200-type transmission electron microscopy (TEM) from FEI and Hitachi company, respectively. The structure of the materials was characterized by the SmartLab 9kW X-ray diffractometer. The elements and functional groups of the materials were analyzed by advanced Fourier Transform infrared spectrometer (6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi) from ThermoFisher. The fluorescence spectrum was measured by Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence quantum efficiency was measured by Edinburgh FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence phosphorescence spectrometer. 2.4 Battery test Cyclic voltammetry was performed on a Biologic electrochemical workstation within a voltage window of 0.01- 3 V at a scan rate of 0.1 mV sâ€“1. Electrochemical impedance was also tested at the Biologic workstation, with AC amplitude 5 mV, frequency range from 0.01 to 105 Hz, and voltage as the open-circuit voltage of the button cell. The test of rate performance and cycle performance of the battery was carried out on LANDCT2000, the voltage window was 0.01-3 V, and the electrode mass was calculated according to the total mass of the composite material. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Electrochemical oxidation of carbonized corncob A typical preparation process of CDs was conducted with carbonized corncob (CC-800) as electrode and 1 mol Lâ€“1 KOH as electrolyte for electrochemical oxidation etching under 1 A constant current for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 1, the electrolyte rapidly turns to yellow brown and black after 1 h, indicating the high preparation efficiency of this method. The prepared CDs have good dispersion in water and no obvious precipitation even after 6 months. By irradiation from the side of glass bottle through the dispersion using a laser light, the obvious Tyndall effect can be observed, showing colloidal characteristics. As can be seen from the digital photo of the cross-section (Fig. 2a), the corncob is composed of three parts from the inside to outside, a soft center, a hard sheath and a rough shell with a vertical sheet structure. The carbonized corncob was examined by SEM. It is found that all parts of the carbonized corncob show porous structures, and the central part has a sponge-like structure (Fig. 2b), which is composed of many chambers with a diameter of 50-160 Î¼m. The sheath is also composed of chambers with thicker walls, and these chambers are connected by micropores on the walls, forming a three-dimensional interconnecting network system (Fig. 2c). The outermost lamellar walls also have developed pore structure (Fig. 2d). This enables the electrolyte to fill the interior of the carbonized corncob during the electrochemical oxidation process, and oxidation reactions can occur both inside and outside, guaranteeing the efficient electrochemical oxidation. The structural evolution of carbonized corncob due to oxidative etching during electrochemical reaction was also analyzed and characterized. It was found that after 1 h of electrochemical reaction, the overall structure of carbonized corncob remained nearly intact (Fig. 2e), except the central part which suffers the most serious etching and almost disappears. The outermost flake structure also suffers a certain degree of etching, while the middle sheath is only slightly etched. These results show that the electrochemical oxidation process of carbonized corncob is not uniform due to the structure and composition difference of CC-800. SEM was further used to analyze the microstructural changes of carbonized corncob during the electrochemical process, and it was found that the innermost central part became riddled with holes after electrochemical etching (Fig. 2f). The thinner wall of each chamber was covered with holes left after etching, and the outermost flake wall was also etched with a large area (Fig. 2h), exposing the inner pore structure. On the other hand, the sheath of the corncob presents different etching phenomena (Fig. 2g). This part is not seriously etched by the electrolyte; only a gradual etching is observed with most of the structure preserved, which is beneficial for maintaining the conductivity of the electrode material for long-term electrochemical reaction. In conclusion, the unique porous structures of natural corncob make carbonized corncob possess high reaction efficiency and long-term stability without further processing treatment after carbonization, which make them very suitable raw material for the preparation of CDs by electrochemical method. Fig. 3a shows a digital photo of CDs powder (about 143.7 mg) prepared from carbonized corncob (about 1.8 g) after 1 h of electrochemical oxidation. The CDsâ€™ preparation rate can be easily improved by increasing the number of carbonized corncob electrodes. Fig. 3b shows CDs aqueous dispersion about 2 L with a concentration of 1 mg mLâ€“1. Fig. 3c-d is a typical TEM image of CDs. Noticeably, the size of as-prepared CDs is relatively uniform, in the range of 1.0-4.0 nm. Fig. 3e is an infrared photograph of carbonized corncob (800 Â°C) with about 10% immersed in the electrolyte. Under the force of capillary, the electrolyte represented by the blue and purple part continuously fills the carbonized corncob with time, indicating that the electrochemical reaction will occur on both the internal and external surfaces of the carbonized corncob. This is an important reason contributing to the efficiency of carbonized corncob in preparing CDs. Fig. 1 Illustration diagram of carbon dot preparation process Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 Fig. 2 (a) Cross-section of corncob; (b-d) SEM images of carbonized corncob before etching :(b) center; (c) sheath; (d) sheet; (e) Longitudinal section of carbonized corncob after etching; (f-h) SEM images of carbonized corncob after etching :(f) center; (g) sheath; (h) sheet Fig. 3 (a) Digital picture of CDs powder; (b) Aqueous dispersion of CDs; (c-d) TEM images of CDs; (e) Infrared image of carbonized corncob with tail immersed in electrolyte 3.2 Structure and properties of CDs In the as-prepared CDs, the typical luminescence wavelength changes with the changing wavelength of the excitation light, as shown in Fig. 4a. The inset shows the photo of water dispersion of CDs under sunlight and ultraviolet light at 365 nm. The fluorescence quantum efficiency is 10.16%. X- ray diffractometer (XRD) was used to characterize the structure of CDs and carbonized corncob (Fig. 4b). Compared with carbonized corncob, the diffraction peak intensity of CDs at about 26o (002) was significantly weaker, while the diffraction peak at about 43o (100) disappeared completely, indicating that the carbon disorder increased after CDs were formed from the carbonized corncob[18]. Fig. 4c shows the Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of CDs and carbonized corncob. The absorption peaks of CDs and carbonized corncob at 3430 cmâ€“1, corresponding to Oâ€“H stretching vibration, and the absorption peaks of CDs at 1 580 cmâ€“1 and 1 430 cmâ€“1 may be derived from C=C stretching vibration on the carbon skeleton. The absorption peaks at 1 350 cmâ€“1 and 1 250 cmâ€“1 may be attributed to Câ€“H stretching vibration and Câ€“O epoxy bond stretching vibration, respectively[9,24]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further confirms that CDs contain abundant oxygen functional groups. Fig. 4d shows the XPS spectrum of CDs and carbonized corncob. Compared with the oxygen content about 9.7 at% of carbonized corncob, the oxygen content of CDs is as high as 40.73 at%. In the C1s spectrum of CDs and carbonized corncob (Fig. 4e-4f), peaks at 284.6, 286.1, 287.8 and 289.0 eV can be seen, corresponding to C=C, Câ€“O, C=O and â€“Oâ€“C=O bonds, respectively[9,25]. The content of various oxygen functional groups in CDs is much higher than that of carbonized corncob. The results indicate that the electrochemical process endows CDs with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, which are conducive to the dispersion of CDs in water. 3.3 Influencing factors on CDs preparation Electrolyte and electrode materials are the key factors affecting the electrochemical reaction. The effect of electrolyte pH and carbonization temperature of raw corncob on the electrochemical preparation of CDs were investigated. By using different electrolytes, such as 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 KOH, 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 NaOH, 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 LiOH, 0.1 mol Lâ€“1 K2SO4, 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 HNO3, 0.1 mol Lâ€“1 H2SO4, 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 H3PO4, 0.2 mol Lâ€“1 HCl, Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 Fig. 4 (a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs; (b) XRD patterns of CDs and CC-800; (c) FTIR spectra of CDs and CC-800; (d) XPS survey spectra of CDs and CC-800; (e) XPS spectra of C1s for CDs; (f) XPS spectra of C1s for CC-800 etc., it was found that the CDs preparation rates per gram of carbonized corncob were about 31.2, 28.0, 25.4, 2.3, 4.8, 1.9, 1.6 and 1.2 mg hâ€“1 Aâ€“1 (Fig. 5a), respectively, indicating that alkaline environment was a necessary condition for the preparation of CDs. We suggest a mechanism in which OH- generates oxygen-containing free radicals with strong oxidation capacity under the condition of anodic oxidation [18,26-27], as follows: 2OHâˆ’ âˆ’ 2ð�‘’âˆ’â†’H2O+ 1 2 O2â†‘ ï¼ˆ1ï¼‰ âˆ™OH âˆ’ âˆ’ eâˆ’ â†’âˆ™ OH ï¼ˆ2ï¼‰ âˆ™OH+OH âˆ’â†’âˆ™O+H2O+ð�‘’âˆ’ ï¼ˆ3ï¼‰ Oxygen-containing free radicals etch the carbon electrode and break the C-C bond. The total anode reaction was as follows: 2OHâˆ’â†’H2O+ 1 2 O2â†‘+2ð�‘’âˆ’ ï¼ˆ4ï¼‰ Cx+OH - â†’CxOH+e- ï¼ˆ5ï¼‰ Cx+4OH - â†’Cxâ€“1+CO2â†‘+2H2O+4ð�‘’âˆ’ ï¼ˆ6ï¼‰ Cx+3OH - â†’Cx-2COOH+CO2â†‘+H2O+3ð�‘’âˆ’ ï¼ˆ7ï¼‰ In the electrolyte with high concentration of OH-, the formation rate of Â·OH is accelerated, which improves the preparation efficiency of CDs. Similarly, the strong alkaline environment can promote the formation of CDs in organic matter[12]. It is found that carbonized corncob at 600 Â°C and below is hardly conductive. The voltage-time curve of electrochemical reaction of carbonized corncob from 700 Â°C to 1 000 Â°C under 1 A constant current is shown in Fig. 5b. The higher the carbonization temperature, the smaller the resistance of the material, and thus the smaller the reaction voltage under constant current. The resistance of corncob carbonized at 700 Â°C is relatively large, while the resistance of corncob carbonized at 800, 900 and 1 000 Â°C is relatively small and not far off from each other. In addition, the working voltage tends to be stable during the reaction without large fluctuations, which may be due to the stability of the sheath of carbonized corncob during the electrochemical reaction (Fig. 2g). The conductivity of the material is consistent, indicating that this method for preparing CDs has stable technological conditions. The size of CDs is also found to depend on the carbonization temperature. As can be seen from Fig. 5c, the higher the carbonization temperature, the sharper the (002) peak, indicating that the degree of graphitization increases with the increasing carbonization temperature. Fig. 5d-g shows the TEM images of CDs prepared by electrochemical oxidation etching of corncobs carbonized at different temperatures, and Fig. 5h-k shows the corresponding statistics of CDs particle size. The average sizes of CDs prepared from corncobs carbonized at 700, 800, 900 and 1 000 Â°C are 1.91, 2.12, 3.37 and 4.14 nm, respectively, indicating that with the increase of carbonization temperature, the growth of graphite microcrystals in carbonized corncobs becomes larger, and the size of as-obtained CDs also increases[9]. However, no obvious fluorescence color size-dependence of CDs has been observed. Considering the green fluorescence color characteristics, it is suggested that the luminescence mechanism of CDs may be mainly caused by surface defects[28]. 3.4 Preparation and sodium storage properties of CDs/rGO composite aerogel Due to its large specific surface area and abundant defects, rGO is an excellent electrode material for sodium ion battery, with abundant sodium storage active sites and good electrical conductivity. However, the serious self-stacking of rGO layers limits the exposure of active sites and the transport of electrolyte through its porous network[10]. It can be assumed that, using the as-prepared CDs as a low-cost additive can restrict the self-stacking of the rGO material and thus enhance its sodium storage performance. Fig. 6a shows a digital photo of CDs/rGO composite aerogel with different CDs dosage Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 Fig. 5 (a) Preparation rates of CDs with different electrolytes; (b) Volt-time curve of electrochemical preparation of CDs from CC-T; (c) XRD patterns of CC-T; TEM images of CDs from CC-T: (d) 700 â„ƒ, (e) 800 â„ƒ, (f) 900 â„ƒ, (g) 1000 â„ƒ; (h-k) CDs size statistics from CC-T: (h) 700 â„ƒ, (i) 800 â„ƒ, (j) 900 â„ƒ, (k) 1 000 â„ƒ Fig. 6 (a) Digital photos of CDs/rGO aerogel with different contents of CDs; (b) TEM of rGO; (c) TEM of CDs/rGO-4 added. From left to right, the mass ratio of CDs to GO is 0.5:1, 0.25:1, 0.125:1 and 0:1. It is clearly evident that the volume of CDs/rGO composite aerogel also increases significantly with the increase in quantity of CDs added. Fig. 6b and Fig. 6c show the TEM images of rGO and CDs/RGO-4, respectively, indicating that CDs are evenly loaded on the rGO layer. The CDs/rGO composite aerogel was assembled into CR2016 button cell to test its electrochemical performance. Fig. 7a shows the CV curve obtained for CDs/RGO-4-H at 0.1 mV sâ€“1. A peak emerged at about 0.1 V during both positive and negative scanning processes, which can be attributed to the release/embedding of sodium ions. There is a peak around 0.7 V in the first turn, which disappears during the subsequent 2-3 scan cycles, suggesting the formation of SEI film and some other irreversible reactions. In addition, there is a wide peak around 2.3 V, which may arise from the pseudo capacitance contribution of oxygen functional groups [29]. Fig. 7b shows the charge and discharge test curve of CDs/rGO-4-H at 0.1 A gâ€“1, showing a sodium storage behavior close to that of the pseudo capacitor[30]. The rate capabilities of pure CDs-H, pure rGO-H, CDs/rGO-8-H, CDs/rGO-4-H, and CDs/rGO-2-H are shown in Fig. 7c. CDs/rGO-4-H exhibits the highest specific capacity. When the current density is 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 A gâ€“1, the specific capacity of CDs/rGO-4-H is 368, 359, 354, 329, 307, 295, 268 mAh gâ€“1, respectively. Compared with CDs/rGO-4-H, CDs-H, pure rGO-H, CDs/rGO-8-H and CDs/rGO-2-H show reversible specific capacities of 25.5, 251, 260 and 284 mAh gâ€“1 at 0.1 A gâ€“1, respectively. With the increase of CDs content, the specific capacity first increases and then decreases. We attribute it to the possibility that with the increase of CD dosage, more rGO layers are supported and separated by CDs, resulting in abundance of microchannels for electrolyte flow and exposure of active sites for sodium storage[13]. However, excessive CDs content may reduce the overall conductivity of the material. Electrochemical reaction kinetics of different materials were analyzed by electrochemical impedance test (EIS). As can be seen from Fig. 7d, the ion transfer resistance and charge transfer resistance of pure CDs and pure rGO are relatively large, which may be due to their poor contact between particles and excessive stacking of sheets, respectively. After the combination of rGO and CDs, both the ion and charge transfer capacity of the materials are remarkably improved. It reflects the role of CDs in regulating material structure. CDs/rGO-4-H is firstly stabilized at 0.1 A gâ€“1 for 5 cycles, the Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 Fig. 7 (a) CV curves of CDs/rGO-4-H; (b) the charge-discharge curves of CDs/rGO-4-H; (c) Ratio performance of materials with different CDs and rGO composite ratio; (d) Nyquist impedance plot of materials with different CDs and rGO composite ratios; (e) Cyclic performance of CDs/rGO-4-H at 1 A g-1 Table 1 Comparison of the results in this study with reported performance of the graphene based cathode materials for sodium-ion batteries Sample Rate capability (mAh gâˆ’1)/ current density (A gâˆ’1) Cyclic stability (mAh gâˆ’1)/ current density (A gâˆ’1) Ref. CDs/rGO aerogel 359.0 / 0.1 268.0 / 5 263.3 / 1 after 1000 cycles This work Expanded graphite 284 / 0.02 91 / 0.2 136 / 0.1 after 2000 cycles [31] Graphene oxide nanosheets 220 / 0.03 105 / 5 150 / 0.1 after 300 cycles [32] Reduced graphene oxide 509 / 0.1 196 / 5 250 / 1 after 1000 cycles [30] Graphene oxide paper 290.5 / 0.05 22.4 / 2 210 / 1 after 250 cycles [33] Crumpled graphene paper 183 / 0.1 61 / 8 120 / 1 after 500 cycles [34] N-carbon dots pillared Graphene blocks 520 / 0.02 118 / 10 125 / 10 after 10000 cycles [13] 3D interconnected hollow channel reduced Graphene oxide 329.8 / 0.1 163.9 / 2 166.8 / 1 after 1000 cycles [35] Holey graphene oxide 365 / 0.1 131 / 10 163 / 2 after 3000 cycles [36] Nitrogen-rich graphene hollow Microspheres 253.8 / 0.1 66.7 / 20 77.8 / 10 after 8000 cycles [37] Nitrogen-doped Graphene foams 1057.1 / 0.1 137.7 / 5 594 / 0.5 after 500 cycles [38] Graphene nanowires anchored to 3D Graphene foam 497 / 0.0375 203 / 7.5 291 / 0.375 after 1000 cycles [39] Rui-lin Li et al. / New Carbon Materials, 2023, 38(2): 347-355 capacity was 263.3 mAh gâ€“1 and the capacity retention rate was 95.5% (Fig. 7e) after 1 000 cycles at 1 A gâ€“1, indicating that this material has excellent cyclic stability. Compared with other graphene-based materials, CDs/rGO-4-H showed high sodium storage performance (Table 1). 4 Conclusions In this work, the high efficiency and controllable preparation of CDs was achieved by carbonization and electrochemical oxidation of corncob, which is one of the most abundant agricultural by-products. Electrochemical oxidation etching of carbonized corncob can produce CDs in large quantities. The natural multi-channel and multi-cavity structures of corncob expand the oxidation reaction area. With 1 mol Lâ€“1 KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte under 1 A constant flow, the CDs preparation rate per gram of carbonized corncob can reach as high as 79.8 mg hâ€“1. The CDs were thoroughly characterized to understand the mechanism of their formation and optimize the reaction conditions. Subsequently, CDs/rGO composite aerogel were prepared in which CDs effectively inhibited the self-stacking of rGO and greatly improved the sodium storage performance of composite aerogel. Using this aerogel as active material, a high sodium storage capacity of 263.3 mAh gâ€“1 could be maintained even after 1 000 cycles at a rate of 1 A gâ€“1. This work demonstrates a new method for preparing CDs on a large scale using cheap and abundant natural precursors, which can find applications in diverse research and commercial sectors. Acknowledgements This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172038, 22179017, U1610105). References [1] Hu C, Li M, Qiu J, et al. item-info.ce:copyright: Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots from carbonized corncobs by electrochemical oxidation and their application in Na-batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Liaoning Key Laboratory for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals
Liaoning Key Laboratory for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Liaoning Key Laboratory for Energy Materials and Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention due to their high specific surface area, good dispersion, abundant surface functional groups, low biotoxicity and photoluminescence. However, their preparation on a large-scale is still a great challenge because of the high cost and environmental problems, and this seriously limits their practical applications. Carbonized corncobs were used as the starting material for the preparation of the CDs by electrochemical oxidation. Their natural porous structures with well-developed channels allow the electrode to be filled with electrolyte, and the electrochemical oxidation takes place both on the inside and outside surfaces of the corncob, achieving a CD output of 79.8 mg h−1 per gram of electrode material at 1 A. The CDs were combined with graphene oxide (GO) to produce CD/rGO composite aerogels by a hydrothermal method. After heat treatment at 600 °C, the materials obtained were used as the anode in a sodium ion battery, which had a capacity of 263.3 mAh g−1 after 1 000 cycles at 1 A g−1. This work suggests a new way to prepare CDs and possibly expand their range of application.
